---
title: "Chapter 4: Biogeochemistry and Geographic Variation in Fish Populations"
subtitle: "(working title)"
author:
  - name: Cali Pfleger
    email: calipfleger@ucsb.edu
date: last-modified
bibliography: literature/bibliography.bib
---

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple" icon=false}
🎞 **[View this chapter as a slideshow →](presentations/chapter4-slides.html)**
:::

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple" icon=false}
📅 **[View the living progress timeline →](presentations/chapter4-progress.html)** — auto-updated nightly with new figures + daily summary.
:::

::: {.callout-warning appearance="simple"}
**This chapter is in early planning.** The scientific questions, geographic scope, and datasets are still being finalized in consultation with the dissertation committee. Content below is a scaffold; treat it as a placeholder until this warning is removed.
:::

## Abstract

*In preparation.* This chapter will examine how marine biogeochemical cycles — particularly dissolved oxygen, nutrient availability, and carbonate chemistry — vary geographically, and what that variation implies for the ecology of commercially and ecologically important fish populations. The analysis will integrate observational oceanographic data with population-ecology records to identify regions where biogeochemical stress may drive population-scale responses.

## Introduction

*To be written.* Intended sections: motivation (why biogeochem × fish-ecology now), geographic scope (regional focus TBD), connection to the paleoclimate chapters (2–3) via shared ocean-circulation mechanisms, and research questions.

## Methods

*To be written.* Intended datasets: WOA / GLODAP for biogeochemistry, NOAA stock-assessment records for fish populations, regional model output for dynamical context.

## Results

## Discussion

## Conclusions


<!-- AUTO 2026-04-20 phase-4 -->
::: {.under-review}
The spatial extent and vertical shoaling of oxygen minimum zones (OMZs) in the eastern Pacific constitute a first-order constraint on pelagic fish distribution. Byrne et al. [-@byrne2024pacific] show using satellite telemetry that the Pacific OMZ creates a distributional boundary for shortfin mako sharks in the Eastern North Pacific, with the limit corresponding to the apex of OMZ shoaling in the North Equatorial Current; projected OMZ expansion will progressively compress suitable habitat for aerobically demanding species. Zhou et al. [-@zhou2020spatial] reconstruct bigeye tuna habitat across the Pacific and find that ENSO-driven OMZ fluctuations shift habitat hotspots: El Niño expands high-quality habitat northward while contracting the western equatorial band via oxycline depth changes. Gutiérrez-Bravo et al. [-@gutierrez2024anoxic] sample fish larvae across five oxygen levels in the Eastern Tropical North Pacific OMZ and find the anoxic core nearly devoid of larvae, forcing early stages into the thin oxygenated surface layer — a compression that tightens with OMZ expansion. At broader trophic scales, Le Grix et al. [-@legrix2023extreme] show using a global marine fish model that compound extreme ocean events (not single-driver anomalies) drive extremely low pelagic fish biomass over 78% of the global ocean, with low oxygen in the eastern equatorial Pacific emerging as a key co-driver alongside temperature and primary productivity. Xing et al. [-@xing2023mesoscale] further demonstrate that mesoscale eddies modulate fishing activity in midlatitude oceans through temperature and oxygen gradients at depth. Together, these studies establish OMZ geometry, ENSO-driven thermocline variability, and compound biogeochemical anomalies as a mechanistic bridge between ocean physics and fish population responses.
:::

<!-- AUTO 2026-04-21 phase-4 -->
::: {.under-review}
The relationship between dissolved-oxygen availability and fish distribution is not straightforwardly negative, as early models predicted. Moriarty et al. [@moriarty2020unexpected] monitored Pacific herring (*Clupea pallasii*) and Pacific hake (*Merluccius productus*) alongside their euphausiid prey in Hood Canal, Washington, during seasonal low-dissolved-oxygen episodes and found that pelagic fish abundance — especially herring — *increased* under hypoxia rather than declining, because vertical compression of both prey and predator into the oxygenated surface layer concentrates fish rather than excluding them; this result highlights that the ecological consequences of oxygen depletion depend critically on the specific predator–prey spatial-overlap geometry. Complementing this, Indivero et al. [@indivero2024threshold] develop a metabolic-index framework for the U.S. Pacific Coast that combines the Arrhenius temperature-dependence of oxygen tolerance with species-distribution monitoring data to infer physiological oxygen thresholds without dedicated laboratory experiments; applied to sablefish (*Anoplopoma fimbria*), the framework improved distributional predictions over temperature-only models, though it did not improve predictions for longspine thornyhead (*Sebastolobus altivelis*), indicating that the metabolic-index approach is species-specific. Together these studies caution against assuming a monotonic negative relationship between OMZ expansion and fish population declines, and motivate biogeochemically-aware distributional modelling that accounts for prey availability, species-specific thresholds, and three-dimensional restructuring of trophic interactions.
:::

<!-- AUTO 2026-04-23 phase-4 -->
::: {.under-review}
Three recent studies sharpen the mechanistic picture linking marine biogeochemistry to fish population dynamics in the Eastern Tropical Pacific — the region most directly coupled to ENSO-scale variability relevant to this chapter. Clarke et al. [@clarke2022pelagic] combine a physiological vulnerability index for large pelagic fishes with Eastern Tropical Pacific catch records from 1970 to 2016, demonstrating that ocean warming is the primary driver of changes in catch composition, while oxygen limitation introduces a significant breakpoint in the sea-surface-oxygen–vulnerability relationship in the Ecuador and Galápagos EEZs; the coupling of temperature and OMZ dynamics during El Niño events amplifies this vulnerability, although only the most extreme events produce detectable compositional shifts. Taboada et al. [@taboada2022bigeye] extend this mechanistic framework to bigeye tuna (*Thunnus obesus*) across the entire Pacific basin, showing that three-dimensional biogeochemical forecasts — integrating subsurface oxygen and thermal structure from an Earth system model — outperform surface-only predictors in hindcasting CPUE regime transitions; their analysis reveals four distinct biogeochemical regimes from 1960–2016, with regime switching most pronounced in the eastern Pacific where ENSO drives basin-wide column oxygen variability. Galloso et al. [@galloso2025humboldt] examine six decades of pelagic fish community data in the northern Humboldt Current System, confirming that dissolved oxygen and temperature together explain the long-term compositional shifts, with warmer and less oxygenated conditions driving higher species diversity — consistent with the hypothesis that OMZ expansion compresses and restructures habitat, reshaping community composition across interannual and decadal scales.
:::

## References {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::
